name: Enceladus
description: Enceladus is a giant in Greek mythology, one of the offspring of Gaia (Earth), born from the blood of Uranus after he was castrated by his son Cronus. He is best known for his role in the Gigantomachy, the epic battle between the Giants and the Olympian gods. Like the other Giants, Enceladus was a monstrous figure, often depicted with serpent-like legs. During the Gigantomachy, Enceladus fought against Athena, the goddess of wisdom and warfare. The myths vary, but it is generally believed that Athena either crushed Enceladus with the island of Sicily, or that he was defeated by Zeus's thunderbolt and subsequently buried beneath the volcano Mount Etna. The rumblings and eruptions of Mount Etna are said to be caused by Enceladus's eternal struggles beneath the mountain, a testament to the enduring power and wrath of the defeated Giant. He represents the primal forces of nature and the challenge to the established order of the Olympian gods.
subtitle: A giant defeated by Athena, buried beneath Mount Etna.
appearance: A massive giant with serpent-like legs, often depicted with a fierce and angry expression. He is immense in size, with a muscular build and wild, unkempt hair. Some depictions include armor forged from the earth itself.
habitat: Originally, the plains of Phlegra, where the Gigantomachy took place. Now, buried beneath Mount Etna in Sicily.
behavior: Wrathful, rebellious, and driven by a desire to overthrow the Olympian gods. In his current state beneath Mount Etna, he is depicted as restless and causing the volcano's eruptions.
weaknesses: Vulnerability to Athena's wisdom and strategy

keyMyths.title: The Gigantomachy
keyMyths.description: Enceladus participated in the Gigantomachy, the war between the Giants and the Olympian gods. He fought against Athena, attempting to overthrow the Olympian order and restore the reign of the Titans. His efforts were ultimately unsuccessful, leading to his defeat and imprisonment.

keyMyths.title: Athena's Victory
keyMyths.description: Athena, the goddess of wisdom and warfare, is credited with defeating Enceladus. Various accounts describe her either crushing him with the island of Sicily or using her Aegis to terrify him into retreat, after which Zeus struck him down with a thunderbolt. This cemented the Olympian gods' dominance.

keyMyths.title: The Rumblings of Etna
keyMyths.description: According to legend, Enceladus is buried beneath Mount Etna in Sicily. His restless movements and struggles to escape cause the volcanic eruptions and earthquakes associated with the mountain. This serves as a constant reminder of the Giants' defiance and the power of the gods to contain them.

sources.author: Pseudo-Apollodorus
sources.description: A comprehensive compilation of Greek myths and legends, detailing the Gigantomachy and Enceladus's role.
sources.text: A comprehensive compilation of Greek myths and legends, detailing the Gigantomachy and Enceladus's role.

sources.author: Hyginus
sources.description: A collection of mythological stories that provides alternative accounts of Enceladus's defeat by Athena.
sources.text: A collection of mythological stories that provides alternative accounts of Enceladus's defeat by Athena.

sources.author: Claudian
sources.description: A Latin epic poem that describes Enceladus being buried under Mount Etna and causing volcanic activity.
sources.text: A Latin epic poem that describes Enceladus being buried under Mount Etna and causing volcanic activity.

sources.author: Hesiod
sources.description: Provides background on the birth of the Giants, including Enceladus, as offspring of Gaia.
sources.text: Provides background on the birth of the Giants, including Enceladus, as offspring of Gaia.
